name: Bres
description: Bres, also known as Eochaid Bres, is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, particularly in the Mythological Cycle. He is known as a king of the Tuatha Dé Danann, but his reign was marked by tyranny and misfortune. His parentage is complex, being the son of Eri, a woman of the Tuatha Dé Danann, and Elatha, a Fomorian king. This dual heritage contributed to his downfall. Bres's reign was characterized by oppression and a neglect of the well-being of his people. He forced the Tuatha Dé Danann into servitude, demanding excessive tributes and providing inadequate sustenance. This led to widespread discontent and ultimately, his removal from power. His story serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of just and benevolent leadership. He lacked the traditional virtues expected of an Irish king, such as generosity and hospitality, leading to his ultimate demise.
subtitle: Oppressive King of the Tuatha Dé Danann
appearance: Bres is described as being strikingly handsome, a trait inherited from his Fomorian father, Elatha. Despite his beauty, he lacked the generosity and kindness expected of a king. Some accounts describe him as physically imposing, befitting his role as a ruler, though his inner character was flawed.
habitat: Tara, the seat of the High Kings of Ireland (in mythology)
behavior: Bres was known for his tyrannical behavior, demanding excessive tributes and providing inadequate sustenance for his people. He was inhospitable and lacked the generosity expected of a king. His behavior was motivated by a desire for power and control, but ultimately led to his downfall.

keyMyths.title: The Reign of Bres
keyMyths.description: Bres's reign was marked by oppression and misfortune. He forced the Tuatha Dé Danann into servitude, demanding excessive tributes and providing inadequate sustenance. He was eventually overthrown by Nuada and Lugh, who restored the Tuatha Dé Danann to their former glory. His story illustrates the importance of just and benevolent leadership.
keyMyths.source: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired

keyMyths.title: Bres's Parentage
keyMyths.description: Bres's parentage is complex, being the son of Eri, a woman of the Tuatha Dé Danann, and Elatha, a Fomorian king. This dual heritage contributed to his downfall, as he lacked the traditional virtues expected of an Irish king. His Fomorian blood influenced his tyrannical behavior and his neglect of the well-being of his people.
keyMyths.source: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired

keyMyths.title: The Satire of Cairbre
keyMyths.description: When Bres was unable to provide proper hospitality to the poet Cairbre, Cairbre satirized him, causing boils to erupt on his face and leading to his downfall. This demonstrates the power of satire in ancient Irish society and its ability to undermine even the most powerful rulers. It also highlights the importance of hospitality in Irish culture.
keyMyths.source: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Irish mythological texts, including 'The Second Battle of Mag Tuired', which details Bres's reign and downfall.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Irish mythological texts, including 'The Second Battle of Mag Tuired', which details Bres's reign and downfall.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis

sources.author: John T. Koch
sources.description: A multi-volume encyclopedia covering various aspects of Celtic culture, including mythology, history, and language. Offers insights into the social and political context of Bres's story.
